claim: Sustained transmission of Avian influenza A between humans is not observed.

result.claim: Sustained transmission of Avian influenza A between humans is not observed.
result.reasoning: Multiple papers consistently state that sustained human-to-human transmission of Avian influenza A has not been observed. Paper 253672, while reporting probable person-to-person transmission of H7N9 in a family cluster, explicitly concludes that "The transmissibility was limited and non-sustainable." Paper 27023968 notes that "H5N1 is not yet capable of efficient human-to-human transmission." Similarly, Paper 30928585 states that "Significant human-to-human transmissions have not been observed yet" for H5N1. Finally, Paper 14618675 also confirms that "no sustained human to human transmission" of H5N1 influenza A viruses has been observed. The evidence from these papers, all of which are abstracts and thus represent the core findings, strongly and consistently supports the claim.

evidence.text: The haemagglutinin (HA) protein of influenza facilitates fusion of the viral membrane and genome entry into the host cell.
evidence.source: Mutations in haemagglutinin that affect receptor binding and pH stability increase replication of a PR8 influenza virus with H5 HA in the upper respiratory tract of ferrets and may contribute to transmissibility
